questions.prompt: Which task is the strongest candidate for automation?
options: A one-time check of a banner you'll never test again
Deciding whether a new screen feels intuitive to use
A login regression run on every build, dozens of times a day
Exploring a brand-new feature to discover unexpected behaviour
questions.explanation: Automation pays off on deterministic, high-repeat checks. A regression that runs every build many times a day earns back its build-and-maintain cost; the others are one-off or judgement-based.

options: A test that passes and fails on the same code with no change between runs
A test that always fails because the app is broken
A test written by a junior tester
A test that takes a long time to run
questions.explanation: Flakiness means inconsistent results on identical code — usually from timing, test order, or shared state. It is worse than a gap because it erodes trust in every result the suite produces.

questions.prompt: Which kind of testing should stay manual?
options: Running the same data-driven case across 50 input rows
Exploratory and usability testing — judging whether something feels right
questions.explanation: Exploratory and usability testing depend on human judgement about look, feel, and confusion. Automation only checks what you told it to; it cannot notice that a flow is awkward or a button is ugly.

questions.prompt: When judging the ROI of automating a test, what matters most?
options: Whether the test is fun to write
Whether other teams have automated something similar
The build-and-maintain cost weighed against how often it runs and the cost of the bug it catches
How many lines of code the test takes
questions.explanation: ROI compares the ongoing cost of a test (writing plus maintenance) against its run frequency and the value of the defects it would catch. High-repeat, high-risk checks win; rare one-offs rarely do.
